extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin (DT) system in the AECO domain may involve **multiple digital twin instances** to address the **heterogeneous and complex infrastructure environments**, as deployment challenges arise from the **fragmented nature of models and data**. While the document does not specify exact multiplicities (e.g., 1:1, 1:N, or N:M mappings between physical and digital components), it implies a need for **hierarchical structuring** to manage **centralized vs. decentralized architectures**. Coordination mechanisms would likely involve **real-time synchronization protocols** to ensure consistency across instances, though no explicit standards (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT, or proprietary APIs) are mentioned. The **scope of responsibilities** for each instance would depend on the **granularity of the physical asset representation** (e.g., building-level vs. subsystem-level twins), with potential **modular decomposition** to isolate functional domains (e.g., HVAC, structural integrity, energy management).
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The **Digital Twin constellation** in the AECO domain requires an **orchestration architecture** capable of integrating **static models** (e.g., BIM, CAD, or simulation models) with **runtime data** (e.g., IoT sensor feeds, operational logs, or environmental metrics) to enable **data-driven infrastructure management**. The system likely follows a **cyber-physical system (CPS) architecture**, where **component integration** is achieved through **standardized interfaces** (though not explicitly named in the document). **Resource management** would prioritize **real-time analytics** and **predictive capabilities**, with coordination mechanisms ensuring **consistent state synchronization** across the twin’s lifecycle (design, construction, operation, and refurbishment). The **system-wide coordination** would rely on **modular service-oriented patterns**, allowing dynamic adaptation to **heterogeneous data sources** and **domain-specific requirements** (e.g., energy optimization, structural health monitoring).
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The Digital Twin system in the AECO domain must support **horizontal integration** with **external systems** through **interoperability standards** to address the **fragmented nature of models and data**. While no specific protocols (e.g., IFC, CityGML, or OPC UA) are mentioned, the document implies reliance on **data exchange frameworks** to enable seamless communication between **Digital Twins and enterprise systems** (e.g., ERP, CMMS, or PLM). **Dependencies on external systems** would include **real-time data feeds** (e.g., from IoT sensors, weather APIs, or utility grids) and **legacy infrastructure models** (e.g., BIM or GIS data). Integration patterns would likely involve **hybrid architectures**, combining **cloud-based services** for scalability with **edge computing** for low-latency processing. The system may also require **federated Digital Twin constellations**, where multiple twins (e.g., for different buildings or subsystems) **collaborate** via **standardized APIs** or **event-driven architectures** to support **cross-domain optimization** (e.g., smart city initiatives).
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin (DT) system leverages a **model-driven deployment framework** rooted in **Building Information Modeling (BIM)** to address its static limitations by introducing dynamic, life-cycle-aware representations. The framework supports **geometric models** (inherited from BIM) and **behavioral models** (enriched with **Building Control System (BCS) components**), including **sensors, actuators, and controllers**, to enable unified semantic foundations. **Data components** are structured through **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) principles**, where enriched BIM models are transformed into deployable DT artifacts via automated extraction processes. The system integrates **real-time data exchange capabilities** to synchronize physical building states with their digital counterparts, ensuring continuous bidirectional synchronization. **Model relationships** are established through a **modular architecture** with standardized extension points, enabling the integration of **custom services and analytics** atop the core framework. Data management is implicit in the framework’s ability to generate deployable artifacts with minimal manual intervention, while **interactive visualization** interfaces provide immersive monitoring and control. The framework’s extensibility allows for evolving operational requirements, supporting both **static (geometric) and dynamic (behavioral/analytical) model types** in a cohesive constellation.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The Digital Twin system incorporates **sensors** as part of the **Building Control System (BCS) components**, which are modeled and configured by **Building Control Engineers** within the BIM environment. These sensors are integrated into the **enriched BIM models** to enable real-time data collection from the physical infrastructure. While the document does not specify exact **sensor types, spatial distribution, sampling frequencies, or transmission rates**, it implies that the framework supports **automated extraction of sensor data** for synchronization with the digital twin. The system relies on **real-time data exchange capabilities** to ensure continuous monitoring, though precise technical specifications (e.g., accuracy, precision, or protocols) are not detailed.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The Digital Twin framework addresses **model fidelity** by bridging the gap between **static BIM representations** and **dynamic DT requirements**, ensuring that enriched BIM models (with BCS components) serve as a **semantic foundation** for accurate digital replicas. **Validation and verification** are implicit in the **automated generation of deployable DT artifacts**, which minimizes manual intervention and reduces human-induced errors. **Uncertainty sources** are mitigated through the framework’s **real-time synchronization** between physical and digital systems, though explicit quantification methods (e.g., statistical error modeling) are not specified. **Quality assurance** is embedded in the **modular architecture**, where standardized extension points allow for **custom services and analytics** to validate and refine model accuracy. The framework’s reliance on **MDE principles** ensures structured representations, while **interactive visualization** interfaces enable stakeholders (e.g., Facility Managers) to cross-validate physical and digital states.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The **physical-to-virtual interaction** in this Digital Twin (DT) system is characterized by **real-time data exchange** between the physical building infrastructure and its digital counterpart, ensuring continuous synchronization. The data flows include **sensor telemetry, operational metrics (e.g., energy consumption, environmental conditions), and device status updates** from IoT-enabled building systems (e.g., HVAC, lighting, security). The framework supports **bidirectional communication** via standardized extension points, enabling integration with **BIM-enriched models** (e.g., Revit, ArchiCAD) for enriched semantic data. **Transmission frequencies** are implied to be **continuous or event-triggered** (e.g., threshold breaches, scheduled intervals) to maintain operational relevance. **Data preprocessing** occurs at the edge or within the DT framework to filter noise, normalize formats, and validate consistency before integration. **Data quality assurance** is ensured through **runtime validation mechanisms** and **low-overhead updates** (as per maintainability NFRs). The integration mechanism leverages the **modular architecture** to dynamically map physical data streams to digital representations, facilitating seamless interoperability with heterogeneous systems (e.g., PLCs, SCADA, or proprietary building management systems).
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The **virtual-to-physical interaction** in this DT system involves **control commands** generated by the digital twin and transmitted to physical building systems for execution. **Command types** include **adjustment signals (e.g., HVAC setpoints, lighting schedules), alerts (e.g., maintenance triggers), and automated responses (e.g., emergency shutdowns)**. The framework enforces **structured control message formats** (e.g., JSON/XML) to ensure compatibility with integrated systems. **Validation and authentication** mechanisms are implied to be embedded within the **runtime configuration management** system, ensuring only authorized commands are executed. **Feedback systems** provide **closed-loop confirmation** via **acknowledgment signals** from physical devices, which are relayed back to the DT for real-time monitoring. **Conflict resolution** is handled through **priority-based arbitration** (e.g., safety overrides preempting comfort adjustments) and **consistency checks** against operational constraints. **Command execution confirmation** is achieved via **bidirectional handshakes** between the DT and physical actuators, ensuring atomicity and traceability of control actions.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The **technical network infrastructure** for this DT system is designed to support **interoperability across heterogeneous systems** (as per NFRs) and leverages a **modular, service-oriented architecture**. The **communication protocols** are not explicitly specified but are implied to include **industry-standard protocols** such as **MQTT (for IoT telemetry), OPC UA (for industrial interoperability), and REST/GraphQL (for service integration)**. The **network topology** follows a **hybrid architecture**, combining **edge computing nodes** (for low-latency local processing) and a **central cloud/on-premise hub** (for global coordination). **Bandwidth requirements** are optimized for **real-time data streams**, with **latency constraints** enforced to ensure sub-second synchronization for critical operations (e.g., safety systems). **Reliability and redundancy** are achieved through **failover mechanisms** and **redundant data paths**, while **security protocols** include **encryption (TLS), access control (RBAC), and anomaly detection** to mitigate cyber threats. The infrastructure is designed to accommodate **scalable, plug-and-play integration** of new devices or services without disrupting existing operations.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The **deployment infrastructure** for this DT system supports **multi-modal hosting platforms**, including **cloud (scalable, centralized), edge (low-latency, localized), and on-premise (secure, proprietary)** environments. **Computational requirements** are dynamically allocated based on workload demands, with **scalability mechanisms** enabling horizontal scaling (e.g., Kubernetes clusters) and vertical scaling (e.g., auto-scaling VMs) to handle peak loads. **Storage requirements** are optimized for **high-velocity data ingestion** (e.g., time-series databases for sensor data) and **long-term archival** (e.g., object storage for BIM models). The framework supports **containerized deployment models** (e.g., Docker, Kubernetes) for **modular service integration** and **runtime configuration management**, allowing adjustments without full redeployment. **Availability and reliability** are assured through **high-uptime SLAs**, **automated failover**, and **disaster recovery protocols**, ensuring continuous operation even during partial infrastructure failures. The deployment is tailored to the **AECO domain**, bridging static BIM representations with dynamic operational needs.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages:
The Digital Twin (DT) lifecycle in the proposed model-driven deployment framework spans from **initial device modeling** to **runtime operation**, addressing the gap between static Building Information Modeling (BIM) representations and dynamic DT requirements. Key phases include:
  1. **Device Modeling and Configuration**: Building Control Engineers define **sensors, actuators, and controllers** within the BIM environment, enriching static BIM models with **semantic foundations** for Building Control Systems (BCS) components. This phase leverages **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)** principles to create structured representations.
  2. **Transformation and Artifact Generation**: The framework extracts structured data from enriched BIM models and **automatically generates deployable DT artifacts** (e.g., executable models, service configurations) with **minimal manual intervention**, ensuring alignment between physical and digital representations.
  3. **Deployment and Synchronization**: The DT is deployed, enabling **real-time data exchange** between the physical building and its digital twin via continuous synchronization mechanisms. This phase supports **immersive visualization** for monitoring and control.
  4. **Runtime Operation and Extension**: The DT operates dynamically, with **modular architecture** and **standardized extension points** allowing Facility Managers to monitor system states and DT Engineers to integrate **custom services, plugins, or analytics** to evolve functionality. Governance is implicit through **role-based access** (e.g., Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers, DT Engineers) and **modular service integration** to ensure operational consistency and adaptability.
  Transition management between phases is facilitated by **MDE-driven transformations**, ensuring seamless progression from static BIM models to dynamic DT artifacts while maintaining traceability and interoperability.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The twinning process in this framework is **model-driven**, leveraging **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)** principles to bridge the gap between static BIM representations and dynamic Digital Twin (DT) requirements. Key aspects include:
  - **Methodologies**: The process begins with **device modeling** in BIM, where Building Control Engineers define **sensors, actuators, and controllers** using a **semantic foundation** to address BIM’s limitations in representing BCS components. This is followed by **automated transformation** of enriched BIM models into structured representations, enabling the generation of **deployable DT artifacts** (e.g., executable models, service configurations) with minimal manual intervention.
  - **Quality Assurance and Testing**: The framework ensures **real-time data exchange** between physical and digital systems, validating synchronization accuracy through **immersive visualization interfaces** for monitoring and control. Custom services and plugins are integrated via **standardized extension points**, requiring validation to maintain consistency with the core DT architecture.
  - **Version Control and Evolution**: The **modular architecture** supports incremental updates, with DT Engineers developing and integrating **custom services** to extend functionality. Evolution is managed through **role-specific workflows** (e.g., Facility Managers for operational adjustments, DT Engineers for functional extensions) and **automated artifact generation** to maintain alignment with physical system changes.
  - **Runtime Governance**: The framework enforces **runtime synchronization** and **interactive visualization** to support dynamic decision-making, while **modular extension points** allow for adaptive evolution without disrupting core operations.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
The Digital Twin (DT) system provides **decision support capabilities** through **real-time data exchange**, **immersive visualization**, and **custom analytics integration**, enabling stakeholders to derive actionable insights. Key components include:
  - **Types of Insights**: Generated insights span **system state monitoring** (e.g., sensor/actuator performance, environmental conditions) and **control logic validation** (e.g., automated adjustments to building operations). Facility Managers interact with the twin to **operate physical infrastructure**, while Building Control Engineers validate control logic against real-time data.
  - **Analytics and Processing**: The framework supports **custom services and plugins** developed by DT Engineers, which can incorporate **advanced analytics** (e.g., predictive maintenance, energy optimization) or **domain-specific processing** (e.g., fault detection). These services are integrated via **standardized extension points** in the modular architecture.
  - **Decision-Making Processes**: Decisions are driven by **interactive visualization interfaces**, which provide immersive monitoring and control tools. Facility Managers use these interfaces to **adjust operational parameters** in real-time, while Building Control Engineers validate and refine control logic based on synchronized data.
  - **User Interaction Methods**: Stakeholders interact with the DT through **role-specific workflows**:
    - **Facility Managers** monitor system states and execute operational commands via the twin.
    - **Building Control Engineers** define and refine control logic within the BIM environment.
    - **DT Engineers** extend functionality by developing custom services, which are then integrated into the DT’s modular architecture.
  - **Confidence Measures**: While explicit confidence metrics are not detailed, the framework ensures **real-time synchronization** and **automated artifact generation** to minimize discrepancies between physical and digital representations, thereby enhancing the reliability of insights and decisions.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.
block_3_docs_retrieved:
TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime
block_4_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
